comments.text: The penultimate inequality should read $(1/2-o(1))r^2\log r\leq g(r)\leq (2+o(1))r^2\log r$. It is currently missing the $g(r)$ in the middle and so is just the observation that $1/2\leq 2$ ! (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I think the upper bound for $g(r)$ can be improved. observe that $g(r)\le \min\{R(3,k)-2k: R(3,k)/k>r-1\}$. consequently, $g(r)\le (1+o(1))r^2\log(r)$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Tom, the upper bound for $g$ follows from the nice construction from a couple days ago. presumably Shearer's bound will imply a lower bound on $g$. but I'm on vacation at the moment, so I will think more about that Monday.
Argh sorry, too many bounds flying around! I'm away from my laptop now but will update soon. Although doesn't that mean the upper bound is actually more like $2r^2\log r$?

no, because their construction has $n$ vertices and no independent set of size $h_\epsilon(n):=(1+\epsilon)\sqrt{n\log n}$ for all $n>n_0(\epsilon)$ (see Theorem 1.3 of https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.19718). and we have that $\frac{r^2\log(r)}{h_\epsilon(r^2\log(r))}= (1+\Theta(\epsilon))r$, I believe. but I could be flipping something incorrectly too.

for the other direction, observe that $g(r)\ge \min\{n: \text{ there exists an $n$-vertex triangle-free graph with chromatic number } r-2\}$. then Raphael Steiner shared this paper https://epubs.siam.org/doi/10.1137/21M1437573 to me, which implies that $g(r)\ge (1/4-o(1))r^2 \log r$ (this improves upon a more standard application of Shearer's by a factor of two). hopefully I did not messup converting constants. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The result in the linked paper of Davies and Illingworth gives $r-2\leq (2+o(1)) \sqrt{n/ \log n}$ for every triangle-free graph with $n$ vertices and chromatic number $r-2$. I believe it even implies $g(r)=n\geq (1/2-o(1)) r^2 \log r$. Also: I get that $\frac{r^2 \log (r)}{h_\epsilon(r^2 \log(r))}$ is of the order $(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\Theta(\epsilon)) r$ rather than $(1+\Theta(\epsilon))r$, so maybe TFBlooms remark still stands that the upper bound argument actually leads to $g(r)\leq (2+o(1))(\log r) r^2$? Probably I'm messing up constants too.. Will check it again in the morning.
ah yes, $\log(r^2\log(r)) \sim 2 \log(r)$... I forgot how logarithms work.

In Theorem 2.7 of "Extremal graph problems with symmetrical extremal graphs. Additional chromatic conditions" (Discrete Math. 7 (1974), 349–376), Simonovits states the answer is $n^2/4 -g_3(t)n/2 + O(1)$ for an explicit function $g_3(t)$. He attributes this result to his PhD thesis, "On the structure-of extremal graphs". For $t=4$, the preprint "Extremal triangle-free graphs with chromatic number at least four" by Ren-Wang-Wang-Yang gives the exact answer $\lfloor \frac{(n-3)^2}{4}\rfloor+6$ for $n\geq 150$. (These references were located by GPT-5.) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
